enrichment.short_description: TheFork Manager is the restaurant reservation, table management and marketing platform built by TheFork, the Paris-based, TripAdvisor-owned dining brand, for independent restaurants and multi-venue groups.
enrichment.seo_title: TheFork Manager Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how TheFork Manager helps restaurants manage reservations, tables and guest marketing, plus its Performance and Enterprise pricing tiers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TheFork Manager is a restaurant management software product built by TheFork, the Paris-headquartered online dining platform founded in 2007 by Bertrand Jelensperger and Stevan Premutico (originally as LaFourchette) and majority-owned by TripAdvisor since 2014. TheFork Manager gives independent restaurants and restaurant groups a single dashboard to handle reservations arriving from TheFork's own consumer app, TripAdvisor, the Michelin Guide, Google, Instagram and Facebook, alongside a smart digital floorplan for table and shift optimization. The product bundles no-show protection tools such as credit-card guarantees, prepayments and automated reconfirmations, a guest CRM with segmentation and loyalty features, AI-assisted review summarization and guest messaging, and performance analytics benchmarked against comparable venues. It also connects to point-of-sale and CRM systems so guest data and spend history sync automatically. TheFork Manager is sold in two tiers, Performance and Enterprise, both quoted on request rather than published as flat monthly fees; restaurants are instead charged a commission on the average bill for guests who book through TheFork, TripAdvisor or partner channels, while bookings sourced through the restaurant's own website, Instagram or Facebook carry no additional commission. The Enterprise tier adds centralized multi-venue data, cross-selling between locations in a group, tailored email campaign tools and a group-level performance dashboard, aimed at restaurant chains and hospitality groups rather than single sites. Competitors in this category include SevenRooms, OpenTable for Restaurants, Resy OS and Tock, and restaurants often choose TheFork Manager specifically because it plugs into TheFork's large European diner audience, making it strongest for restaurants in France, Italy, Spain and other markets where TheFork has significant consumer reach.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TheFork Manager
paragraphs: TheFork Manager is the business-facing software that restaurants use to run the operational side of taking reservations through TheFork, a European online dining platform founded in Paris in 2007 and majority-owned by TripAdvisor since 2014. Instead of just listing a restaurant for diners to find, TheFork Manager gives the restaurant a single back-office dashboard for managing every reservation, regardless of whether it originated on TheFork's own app, TripAdvisor, the Michelin Guide, Google, the restaurant's own website, or its Instagram and Facebook pages.

The product is aimed at both independent restaurants and multi-location restaurant groups, and it positions itself less as a booking widget and more as an all-in-one operations layer that also touches marketing, guest relationship management and performance reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of TheFork Manager is a smart digital floorplan that helps restaurants optimize table assignments and turn times across services, paired with a centralized reservation inbox that pulls bookings from all connected channels into one view. No-show protection tools, including credit-card guarantees, prepayments and automated reconfirmation messages, are built in to reduce lost revenue from last-minute cancellations.

On the marketing and guest-relationship side, TheFork Manager includes a guest CRM with segmentation for building targeted campaigns and loyalty programs, AI-powered tools that summarize customer reviews and help staff respond to guest messages faster, and performance analytics that benchmark a restaurant's booking trends against similar venues. Integrations with POS and CRM systems keep guest profiles and spend history in sync across systems the restaurant already uses, and a mobile app lets managers adjust availability and bookings from the floor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TheFork Manager does not publish flat monthly prices. It is sold in two tiers, Performance and Enterprise, both quoted after a sales conversation based on the restaurant's size, location and reservation volume. Enterprise adds features aimed at restaurant groups, such as centralized multi-venue data, cross-selling between locations, tailored email campaigns and a group-level performance dashboard.

Instead of a fixed subscription covering all bookings, TheFork Manager charges restaurants a commission calculated as a percentage of the average bill per guest for reservations that come through TheFork, TripAdvisor or other partner channels. Reservations generated through the restaurant's own website, Instagram or Facebook page are not subject to this commission.

features.description: Combines bookings from TheFork, TripAdvisor, the Michelin Guide, Google, and the restaurant's own site and social channels into one dashboard.

features.description: Visual table management that optimizes seating capacity and turn times with real-time availability.

features.title: No-Show Protection
features.description: Credit-card guarantees, prepayments and automated reconfirmations to cut down on last-minute cancellations.

features.title: Guest CRM and Loyalty
features.description: Centralized guest database supporting segmented marketing campaigns and loyalty programs.

features.title: AI-Powered Guest Communication
features.description: AI tools summarize customer reviews and speed up responses to guest messages.

features.title: Performance Analytics
features.description: Benchmarked reporting on booking trends and performance versus comparable restaurants.

features.title: POS and CRM Integrations
features.description: Syncs guest data and spend history with the restaurant's existing point-of-sale and CRM systems.

features.title: Mobile Management App
features.description: Lets managers adjust table availability and bookings from a phone while on the restaurant floor.

pros: Plugs directly into TheFork's large European diner audience for extra bookings
Combines table management with marketing and CRM in one dashboard
No-show tools like prepayments and card guarantees reduce lost revenue
AI tools summarize reviews and speed up guest replies
Enterprise tier suits multi-location restaurant groups
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Commission fees apply to TheFork and partner-sourced bookings
Most valuable in TheFork's core European markets, less established for US-only operators
Some advanced features are gated behind the higher Enterprise tier
Onboarding and integration setup can take time for larger restaurant groups

faqs.question: What is TheFork Manager used for?
faqs.answer: It is restaurant management software for handling reservations, table planning, guest CRM and marketing in one system.

faqs.question: How much does TheFork Manager cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is custom and quoted by TheFork's sales team; reservations from TheFork and partner channels are charged a commission based on average bill per guest.

faqs.question: Does TheFork Manager charge commission on all bookings?
faqs.answer: No. Commission applies to bookings from TheFork, TripAdvisor and partner channels, while bookings from the restaurant's own website, Instagram or Facebook are not commissioned.

faqs.question: Is TheFork Manager only for restaurants that use TheFork app?
faqs.answer: No. It also manages bookings from TripAdvisor, the Michelin Guide, Google, and the restaurant's own website and social pages.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Performance and Enterprise plans?
faqs.answer: Enterprise includes everything in Performance plus multi-venue tools for restaurant groups, such as centralized data, cross-selling and group performance dashboards.

faqs.question: Does TheFork Manager integrate with POS systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, it syncs guest data with point-of-sale and CRM systems the restaurant already uses.

faqs.question: Who owns TheFork Manager?
faqs.answer: It is built by TheFork, a Paris-based company that is majority owned by TripAdvisor.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for TheFork Manager?
faqs.answer: TheFork does not publicly advertise a self-serve free trial; interested restaurants typically request a demo and custom quote.

use_cases.description: A restaurant expands its visibility and bookings through TheFork's and TripAdvisor's diner audiences.

enrichment.short_description: TheHive is a Security Incident Response Platform built by StrangeBee for SOC, CERT and CSIRT teams to triage alerts, manage cases and collaborate on investigations; not to be confused with Apache Hive or unrelated project-management apps also named Hive.
enrichment.seo_title: TheHive Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TheHive is StrangeBee's incident response platform for SOC and CERT teams. See its case management features, Cortex integration and pricing tiers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TheHive is a Security Incident Response Platform originally released in 2016 by Thomas Franco, Nabil Adouani and Jerome Leonard, three former CERT-BDF analysts, as a free open-source alternative for security operations centers, CERTs and CSIRTs. In 2018 the same three creators founded StrangeBee, a Paris-based cybersecurity company, to formalize development and support of TheHive alongside its companion analysis engine Cortex. TheHive gives analysts a shared case-management workspace: alerts imported from SIEMs, email and other detection sources can be triaged and escalated into cases, cases are broken into tasks and observables, and multiple analysts can work the same investigation simultaneously with full audit history. Cortex integration lets teams automatically enrich observables such as IPs, hashes and domains against threat-intel feeds and sandboxes, while MISP integration lets teams import indicators of compromise from MISP events or push their own findings back to sharing communities. With TheHive 5, StrangeBee shifted from a purely open-source model to a mixed one: a free Community edition remains available for on-premises deployments with core incident-response functionality, while Gold and Platinum editions are commercial annual subscriptions priced by number of users and organizations, unlocking advanced automation, larger multi-tenancy and premium support; StrangeBee also offers a fully managed TheHive Cloud Platform, though the Community edition is on-prem only. Earlier open-source releases, TheHive 3 and 4, remain in legacy use, though public availability of those older builds ended in 2025. TheHive is commonly compared to commercial SOAR and case-management tools such as IBM QRadar SOAR and Palo Alto Cortex XSOAR, and its main differentiator is deep, free-tier MISP interoperability plus a large base of community-built Cortex analyzers and responders, with more than 300 integrations built up since 2016.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TheHive
paragraphs: TheHive is a Security Incident Response Platform that gives security teams a shared workspace for managing cases, tasks and observables during an investigation. It began in 2016 as a free, open-source project from three former CERT-BDF analysts, and by 2018 those creators had formalized the project into a company, StrangeBee, based in Paris.

The platform is built for security operations centers, CERTs, CSIRTs and MSSPs that need to move from a raised alert to a documented, collaborative investigation quickly, with tight integration into the broader threat-intelligence ecosystem via MISP and the observable-analysis engine Cortex, both also maintained by StrangeBee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TheHive's core is case and task management: alerts from SIEMs, email reports and other sources can be imported and triaged, then escalated into full cases broken into tasks, with reusable case templates for common incident types. Multiple analysts can work the same case at once, with a full history of changes for accountability and reporting.

Observable enrichment happens through Cortex, which runs analyzers against IPs, file hashes, domains and other indicators using external threat-intel sources and sandboxes, and through responders that can take active containment or notification actions. MISP integration lets teams pull in indicators of compromise from shared MISP events or export their own findings back out, and a library of more than 300 community-built integrations extends the platform further.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TheHive offers a free Community edition for self-hosted, on-premises deployments, covering core case management, alert triage and Cortex/MISP integration for teams that do not need advanced multi-tenancy or premium support.

Above that, TheHive Gold and Platinum are commercial annual subscriptions priced by the number of users and organizations (tenants) supported, available for on-prem or cloud deployment, and StrangeBee also sells a fully managed TheHive Cloud Platform and an MSSP license for partners. None of the paid tiers publish flat prices; StrangeBee quotes them directly based on team size and deployment needs.

features.title: Case and Task Management
features.description: Structures investigations into cases with tasks, templates, and full audit history.

features.title: Alert Triage from SIEM and Email
features.description: Imports alerts from SIEMs, email reports and other sources for analysts to escalate into cases.

features.title: Cortex Observable Enrichment
features.description: Runs automated analyzers against IPs, hashes and domains using threat-intel feeds and sandboxes.

features.title: MISP Threat Intelligence Integration
features.description: Imports IOCs from MISP events and exports findings back to sharing communities.

features.title: Multi-Analyst Real-Time Collaboration
features.description: Multiple analysts can work the same case simultaneously with tracked changes.

features.title: Custom Case Templates
features.description: Reusable templates speed up handling of common incident types.

features.title: 300-Plus Integrations
features.description: A large library of community-built analyzers and responders extends functionality.

features.title: Multi-Tenancy
features.description: Gold and Platinum editions support multiple organizations within one deployment.

pros: Free Community edition covers core incident response workflows for on-prem teams
Deep native integration with MISP and Cortex for threat intel and enrichment
Large community of analyzers and responders built over nearly a decade
Real-time multi-analyst collaboration on the same case
Flexible deployment as on-prem, cloud or MSSP-hosted
cons: Commercial Gold and Platinum pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales quote
Community edition is on-prem only, not available on TheHive Cloud Platform
Self-hosting requires Elasticsearch/Cassandra-class infrastructure and ops know-how
Public availability of legacy TheHive 3 and 4 builds ended in 2025, pushing users toward paid tiers
Steeper learning curve for teams without a dedicated security operations background

faqs.question: What is TheHive used for?
faqs.answer: It is a Security Incident Response Platform used by SOC, CERT and CSIRT teams to manage and investigate security cases.

faqs.question: Is TheHive free?
faqs.answer: The Community edition is free for self-hosted, on-premises use; Gold and Platinum are paid commercial subscriptions.

faqs.question: What is the difference between TheHive and Cortex?
faqs.answer: TheHive is the case-management platform; Cortex is the companion engine that runs analyzers and active responses against observables.

faqs.question: Who makes TheHive?
faqs.answer: TheHive is maintained by StrangeBee, a Paris-based cybersecurity company founded in 2018 by TheHive's original creators.

faqs.question: What is the difference between TheHive Community, Gold and Platinum?
faqs.answer: Community is free and on-prem only with core features; Gold and Platinum are paid subscriptions priced by users and organizations, adding advanced automation and support.

faqs.question: Does TheHive integrate with MISP?
faqs.answer: Yes, TheHive has native integration with MISP for importing and exporting threat indicators.

faqs.question: Can TheHive be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the free Community edition and the paid editions support on-premises self-hosted deployment.

faqs.question: What happened to TheHive 3 and 4?
faqs.answer: Public availability of the legacy TheHive 3 and 4 open-source builds ended in 2025 as StrangeBee moved focus to TheHive 5.

use_cases.title: SOC Alert Triage
use_cases.description: A security operations team imports SIEM alerts into TheHive and triages which ones escalate into full investigations.

use_cases.title: Multi-Analyst Incident Investigation
use_cases.description: A CERT or CSIRT coordinates several analysts working the same case simultaneously with full audit history.

use_cases.title: MSSP Incident Response Service
use_cases.description: A managed security service provider uses TheHive's multi-tenancy to run incident response for multiple client organizations.

enrichment.short_description: Thelia is a French open-source e-commerce platform built on the Symfony framework, maintained by digital agency OpenStudio, offering a free, self-hosted alternative to Shopify or PrestaShop for B2B, B2C and omnichannel stores.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Thelia is a free, open-source French ecommerce platform built on Symfony. See its B2B, omnichannel and marketplace features plus how pricing works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Thelia is a free, open-source e-commerce platform created in 2005 by Cedric Sibaud and Yoan de Macedo and released under an open license in 2006; today it is maintained by OpenStudio, a French web agency also founded in 2005 and headquartered in Clermont-Ferrand with additional offices in Toulouse, Paris and Lyon. Thelia's current major version, Thelia 2, is built on the Symfony PHP framework and licensed under LGPL, giving developers full source-code access and freedom to self-host, modify and extend the platform without licensing fees. The software targets merchants and agencies who need deep customization beyond what closed SaaS platforms like Shopify allow: it exposes a REST API and templating system that support ERP and CRM integration, click-and-collect and drive or curbside pickup, in-store order picking with barcode scanning, B2B storefronts with client-specific pricing and payment terms, multisite management from one back office, product information management, and marketplace connections. A module marketplace with roughly 300 extensions lets teams add payment gateways, shipping carriers and marketing tools, and Thelia reports more than 7,000 live websites running on the platform, concentrated mostly in France and other French-speaking markets. Because Thelia itself is free software, most of the cost for a Thelia store comes from hosting, developer time, premium modules and themes, and optional professional services or support contracts sold by OpenStudio and its partner agencies rather than from Thelia licensing fees. Thelia competes most directly with other open-source carts such as PrestaShop, also French-built, and with WooCommerce, and with hosted platforms like Shopify, and is typically chosen by businesses that want full code ownership, Symfony-based extensibility, or complex B2B and omnichannel logic that off-the-shelf SaaS carts do not handle well out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Thelia
paragraphs: Thelia is a free, open-source e-commerce platform that has been in continuous development since 2005, created by Cedric Sibaud and Yoan de Macedo and now maintained by the French web agency OpenStudio. Its current major version, Thelia 2, is built on the Symfony PHP framework and released under the LGPL license, meaning merchants and developers can self-host, inspect and modify the full source code without paying licensing fees.

Thelia is aimed at businesses and agencies that need more customization and code ownership than closed hosted platforms typically allow, and it has powered thousands of online stores in France and other French-speaking markets over nearly two decades.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Thelia exposes a REST API and flexible templating system that support a wide range of commerce models, including B2B storefronts with client-specific pricing and payment terms, multisite management from a single back office, and product information management for large catalogs.

Omnichannel retailers can use Thelia for click-and-collect and drive or curbside pickup, along with a dedicated order-picking interface featuring barcode scanning, label generation and order verification for in-store fulfillment. A module marketplace of roughly 300 extensions adds payment gateways, shipping carriers, marketplace connections and marketing tools on top of the core platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Thelia itself is free to download, install and use, since it is open-source software released under the LGPL license. There is no subscription fee or per-transaction charge from Thelia for running the core platform.

Real-world costs come from hosting infrastructure, developer time to build and maintain the store, premium modules or themes purchased from the marketplace, and optional professional services or support contracts from OpenStudio or partner agencies, which is how the maintaining company monetizes the ecosystem around the free software.

features.title: Symfony-Based Architecture

features.title: Click and Collect / Drive Fulfillment

features.title: Multisite Management

features.title: Order Picking with Barcode Scanning

features.title: Module Marketplace
features.description: Around 300 community and commercial modules extend payments, shipping and marketing features.

features.title: PIM and Marketplace Connections
features.description: Supports product information management and connections to third-party marketplaces.

pros: Free and open source with full source-code ownership
Built on the well-established Symfony PHP framework
Strong B2B and omnichannel feature set out of the box
Around 300 community and commercial modules available
Nearly two decades of active development and maintenance
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than PrestaShop or WooCommerce internationally
Requires PHP and Symfony development skills to customize and maintain
Hosting, premium modules and support are separate costs
Documentation and community resources are stronger in French than English
Not a fully managed SaaS, so merchants own infrastructure and updates

faqs.question: What is Thelia used for?
faqs.answer: Thelia is an open-source platform for building and running online stores, including B2B, omnichannel and multisite setups.

faqs.question: Is Thelia free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Thelia is free open-source software; costs come from hosting, developer time, premium modules and optional support.

faqs.question: What license is Thelia released under?
faqs.answer: Thelia 2 is released under the LGPL license, after starting under GPL in 2006.

faqs.question: Who maintains Thelia?
faqs.answer: Thelia is maintained by OpenStudio, a French web agency founded in 2005 and headquartered in Clermont-Ferrand.

faqs.question: What framework is Thelia built on?
faqs.answer: Thelia 2 is built on the Symfony PHP framework.

faqs.question: Does Thelia support B2B selling?
faqs.answer: Yes, Thelia supports B2B storefronts with client-specific pricing, payment modes and delivery terms.

faqs.question: How many websites use Thelia?
faqs.answer: Thelia reports more than 7,000 live websites running on the platform, mostly in French-speaking markets.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Thelia and PrestaShop?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, French-built e-commerce platforms; Thelia is built on Symfony with a strong B2B and omnichannel feature set, while PrestaShop has a larger international community.

enrichment.short_description: Theneo is an AI-powered API documentation platform, founded by Maria Doliashvili and Ana Robakidze, that auto-generates, maintains and publishes interactive developer docs from OpenAPI, Postman, GraphQL and other API specs.
enrichment.seo_title: Theneo Review 2026: AI API Documentation Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Theneo auto-generates AI-powered API docs from OpenAPI and Postman specs. Compare its Starter, Business, Growth and Enterprise pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Theneo is an AI-powered API documentation platform built by Theneo Inc, a company founded around 2021 by Maria Doliashvili and Ana Robakidze and graduated from Y Combinator's Winter 2022 batch; the company is headquartered in San Francisco with an engineering team based in Tbilisi, Georgia, and has raised early-stage funding including from Y Combinator. Theneo's core pitch, as covered by TechCrunch in 2022, is bringing Stripe-quality API documentation to teams that lack dedicated technical writers: it ingests OpenAPI/Swagger 2.0 and 3.x specs, Postman collections, GraphQL SDL or introspection, gRPC proto files, SOAP/WSDL and AsyncAPI definitions, then uses AI to generate readable descriptions, keep examples current, and flag documentation that has drifted from the live API. Beyond auto-generation, Theneo offers an interactive API Explorer for testing endpoints directly from the docs, AI-assisted search, automatic changelog generation when specs change, and authenticated developer-hub portals where individual customers can log in, with SSO available, and see only the APIs and SDKs relevant to them. Pricing runs on a freemium model: the Starter plan is free and includes one public and two private projects with a capped number of AI documentation builds and searches; Business is a paid workspace plan at 120 dollars per month billed annually, or 150 dollars month-to-month, adding more projects, custom branding, a custom domain and a developer hub; Growth, at 400 dollars per month annually or 450 dollars monthly, adds multiple developer hubs, custom CSS, advanced security and IP whitelisting; and Enterprise is custom-priced and adds self-hosting, SAML SSO, OAuth 2.0 and custom roles. Theneo is most often compared to Mintlify, ReadMe and Redocly as competing AI-era API documentation tools, differentiating itself primarily through its breadth of supported spec formats and its AI-driven documentation-drift detection.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Theneo
paragraphs: Theneo is an AI-powered platform for generating and maintaining API documentation. Founded around 2021 by Maria Doliashvili and Ana Robakidze and backed by Y Combinator's Winter 2022 batch, the company is headquartered in San Francisco with much of its engineering team based in Tbilisi, Georgia.

The product is built for teams that need Stripe-quality developer docs without dedicating a full-time technical writer to the job, connecting directly to a codebase or API spec to detect changes and keep documentation accurate over time rather than treating docs as a one-time export.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Theneo supports essentially every major API spec format, including OpenAPI/Swagger 2.0 and 3.x, Postman collections, GraphQL SDL and introspection, gRPC proto files, SOAP/WSDL and AsyncAPI, so teams can bring documentation in from whatever tooling they already use. Its AI features generate readable descriptions and examples, detect when documentation has drifted from the live API, and power an AI-assisted search experience for readers.

For teams selling APIs to other businesses, Theneo offers authenticated developer-hub portals where each customer signs in, with per-customer logins, email invites and SSO if needed, and sees only the APIs, guides and SDKs relevant to them. An interactive API Explorer lets readers test endpoints directly from the documentation, and automatic changelog generation keeps a record of what changed and when.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Theneo's Starter plan is free and includes one public project, two private projects, and capped usage of its AI documentation builder and search features, making it usable for small or early-stage API projects.

Paid plans scale by workspace: Business costs 120 dollars per month billed annually or 150 dollars month-to-month and adds more projects, custom branding, a custom domain and a developer hub; Growth costs 400 dollars per month annually or 450 dollars monthly and adds multiple developer hubs, custom CSS and advanced security controls; and Enterprise is custom-priced, adding self-hosting, SAML SSO, OAuth 2.0 and custom user roles for large organizations.

features.title: AI-Generated API Documentation
features.description: Automatically generates and updates documentation content from connected API specs.

features.title: Multi-Format Spec Support
features.description: Supports OpenAPI, Postman, GraphQL, gRPC, SOAP/WSDL and AsyncAPI formats.

features.title: Interactive API Explorer
features.description: Lets readers test API endpoints directly from within the documentation.

features.title: Automatic Changelog Generation
features.description: Tracks and documents API changes automatically as specs evolve.

features.title: AI-Powered Search
features.description: AI-assisted search helps readers find relevant documentation faster.

features.title: Authenticated Developer Hubs
features.description: Per-customer portals showing only the APIs and SDKs relevant to each signed-in user.

features.title: Custom Branding and Domains
features.description: Business-tier and above plans support custom branding, SEO settings and custom domains.

features.title: SSO and Enterprise Access Controls
features.description: SAML SSO, OAuth 2.0 and custom roles available on the Enterprise tier.

pros: Free Starter plan is usable for small public or private API projects
Supports a wide range of spec formats beyond just OpenAPI
AI features cut down manual documentation maintenance work
Authenticated per-customer developer hubs are useful for B2B APIs
Y Combinator-backed with active product development
cons: Higher tiers like Growth and Enterprise get expensive for larger teams
Self-hosting is only available on the custom Enterprise tier
AI-generated content still needs human review for accuracy
Smaller brand recognition than established players like ReadMe or Swagger
Some advanced features are workspace-limited rather than per-seat

faqs.question: What is Theneo used for?
faqs.answer: Theneo is used to auto-generate, maintain and publish interactive API documentation from specs like OpenAPI and Postman.

faqs.question: Is Theneo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Theneo has a free Starter plan with limited public and private projects and capped AI usage.

faqs.question: What API spec formats does Theneo support?
faqs.answer: Theneo supports OpenAPI/Swagger, Postman collections, GraphQL, gRPC, SOAP/WSDL and AsyncAPI.

faqs.question: Who founded Theneo?
faqs.answer: Theneo was founded by Maria Doliashvili and Ana Robakidze and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 batch.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Theneo Business and Growth plans?
faqs.answer: Growth adds multiple developer hubs, custom CSS, advanced security and IP whitelisting on top of everything in Business.

faqs.question: Does Theneo offer self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting is available on Theneo's custom-priced Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: How does Theneo compare to Mintlify?
faqs.answer: Both are modern API documentation platforms; Theneo emphasizes AI-driven generation and broad spec-format support, including GraphQL, gRPC and SOAP.

faqs.question: Does Theneo support SSO?
faqs.answer: SAML SSO and OAuth 2.0 are available on Theneo's Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Per-Customer Developer Hubs
use_cases.description: A B2B platform company gives each customer an authenticated hub showing only their relevant APIs and SDKs.

use_cases.title: Multi-Format API Documentation
use_cases.description: An API-first company documents REST, GraphQL and gRPC services together in one platform.

features: Capped AI documentation builder and search usage
Unlimited AI search and doc builder

enrichment.short_description: TheraNest, now operating as Ensora Mental Health under Ensora Health, is a practice management and EHR platform for therapists, counselors, psychologists and social workers, covering scheduling, notes, telehealth and billing.
enrichment.seo_title: TheraNest Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Ensora Rebrand
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TheraNest (now Ensora Mental Health) offers therapy scheduling, notes, billing and telehealth. Compare its Essentials, Advanced and Premier pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TheraNest is a practice management and electronic health record platform for behavioral and mental health providers, founded in 2013 in Birmingham, Alabama by Shegun Otulana. The product was later acquired into Therapy Brands, a portfolio company that private equity firm KKR bought for roughly 1.25 billion dollars in May 2021; in April 2025 Therapy Brands rebranded its whole portfolio to Ensora Health, and TheraNest itself was renamed Ensora Mental Health, though many customers and search listings still refer to it by the original TheraNest name. The platform covers the standard outpatient therapy workflow: appointment scheduling with automated no-show fee enforcement, a secure client portal, calendar sync, structured SOAP notes and a customizable Dynamic Form Builder for intake and progress notes, telehealth video sessions, e-prescribing, insurance billing with clearinghouse connections and ERA posting, and an AI Session Assistant that helps draft session documentation. TheraNest is priced per therapist per month across three tiers: Essentials at 29 dollars covers scheduling, the client portal and basic AI-enhanced notes; Advanced at 59 dollars adds online booking, secure messaging, custom forms and a monthly allotment of free claims processing and reminders; and Premier at 89 dollars adds unlimited claims processing, eligibility checks and reminders plus the Wiley Practice Planner content library. Paying annually reduces the effective monthly price to 27, 54 and 82 dollars respectively. Add-ons include the AI Session Assistant at 35 dollars per provider per month, telehealth for Essentials subscribers at 12 dollars per therapist, and e-prescribing with PDMP checks at 45 dollars per therapist plus a one-time setup fee. TheraNest competes with SimplePractice, TherapyNotes and TherapyAppointment in the mental-health-specific EHR category, and is generally positioned as a mid-market option with strong billing automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TheraNest
paragraphs: TheraNest is a practice management and electronic health record platform built specifically for mental and behavioral health providers, founded in 2013 in Birmingham, Alabama by Shegun Otulana. It later became part of Therapy Brands, which KKR acquired for roughly 1.25 billion dollars in 2021, and in April 2025 the whole portfolio, including TheraNest, was rebranded as Ensora Health, with the product itself now marketed as Ensora Mental Health.

The platform is designed for solo therapists, group practices and behavioral health organizations that need scheduling, clinical documentation, telehealth and insurance billing in one system, rather than juggling separate tools for each function.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality covers appointment scheduling with automated no-show fee enforcement, a secure client portal, calendar sync, and clinical documentation through structured SOAP notes and a Dynamic Form Builder for custom intake and progress note templates. Telehealth video sessions and e-prescribing with PDMP checks round out the clinical side.

On the administrative side, TheraNest handles insurance billing with clearinghouse integration and ERA posting, and its AI Session Assistant helps clinicians draft session documentation faster. Higher tiers add the Wiley Practice Planner content library and unlimited claims processing and eligibility checks, aimed at practices that want to minimize manual billing work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TheraNest is priced per therapist per month across three published tiers: Essentials at 29 dollars, Advanced at 59 dollars, and Premier at 89 dollars, each adding more billing automation, forms and content on top of the last. Paying annually includes one month free, bringing the effective monthly cost down to 27, 54 and 82 dollars respectively.

Several features are sold as add-ons rather than bundled into the base price: the AI Session Assistant costs 35 dollars per provider per month, telehealth for Essentials subscribers costs 12 dollars per therapist per month, and e-prescribing with PDMP checks costs 45 dollars per therapist per month plus a one-time setup fee. All plans include HIPAA compliance, unlimited clients, and complimentary billing and scheduling staff roles.

features.description: Built-in video telehealth, available as a paid add-on on the Essentials plan.

features.title: Insurance Billing and Clearinghouse Integration
features.description: Claims processing, eligibility checks and ERA posting through connected clearinghouses.

features.title: AI Session Assistant
features.description: AI tool that helps clinicians draft session documentation faster.

features.title: E-Prescribing with PDMP Checks
features.description: Electronic prescribing with prescription drug monitoring program checks, sold as an add-on.

features.title: Wiley Practice Planner Content
features.description: Included on the Premier plan, providing treatment-planning content for clinicians.

pros: Transparent, published per-therapist pricing across three tiers
Purpose-built for mental and behavioral health workflows, not generic medical EHR
Insurance billing automation reduces manual claims work
AI Session Assistant speeds up documentation
Annual billing offers a meaningful discount over monthly pricing
cons: Telehealth and e-prescribing are paid add-ons on lower tiers
Brand is transitioning to Ensora Mental Health, which can cause confusion
Higher tiers are needed to unlock unlimited billing automation
AI and e-prescribing add-ons increase the effective monthly cost significantly
Owned by a large private-equity-backed portfolio, which some independent practices may weigh when choosing a vendor

faqs.question: What is TheraNest used for?
faqs.answer: It is practice management and EHR software for therapists covering scheduling, clinical notes, telehealth and insurance billing.

faqs.question: How much does TheraNest cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at 29 dollars per therapist per month for Essentials, up to 89 dollars for Premier, with a discount for annual billing.

faqs.question: Is TheraNest the same as Ensora Mental Health?
faqs.answer: Yes, TheraNest was renamed Ensora Mental Health in April 2025 after its parent company Therapy Brands rebranded as Ensora Health.

faqs.question: Does TheraNest include telehealth?
faqs.answer: Telehealth is included on Advanced and Premier plans and available as a paid add-on for Essentials subscribers.

faqs.question: What is the AI Session Assistant?
faqs.answer: It is an add-on AI tool priced at 35 dollars per provider per month that helps clinicians draft session documentation.

faqs.question: Does TheraNest handle insurance billing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes claims processing, eligibility checks and ERA posting, with usage limits that increase by plan tier.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Essentials, Advanced and Premier?
faqs.answer: Essentials covers core scheduling and notes, Advanced adds online booking and limited free billing usage, and Premier adds unlimited billing automation and the Wiley Practice Planner.

faqs.question: Who owns TheraNest?
faqs.answer: TheraNest is owned by Ensora Health, formerly Therapy Brands, which KKR acquired in 2021.

enrichment.short_description: ThingsBoard is an open-source IoT platform for device management, data collection, processing and visualization, offered as a free self-hosted Community Edition plus paid Professional Edition, Private Cloud and ThingsBoard Cloud tiers.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: ThingsBoard Review 2026: IoT Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ThingsBoard is an open-source IoT platform. Compare its free Community Edition against paid Professional, Private Cloud and ThingsBoard Cloud pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ThingsBoard is an open-source Internet of Things platform for device management, telemetry data collection, rule-based processing and dashboard visualization, first released in 2016 and formalized as a company, ThingsBoard Inc, in 2018, headquartered in New York. The Community Edition is licensed under Apache 2.0, free to self-host with no cap on the number of devices, assets or messages, and includes core building blocks such as an MQTT/HTTP/CoAP-based transport layer, a visual rule engine for processing telemetry and triggering alarms or actions, multi-tenancy, and a library of more than 600 prebuilt, customizable dashboard widgets for building end-user IoT interfaces. Teams that need more than community support can move to the self-managed Professional Edition, sold as a monthly subscription starting around 10 dollars per month for a 10-device Maker plan and scaling to roughly 499 dollars per month for 1,000 devices on the Business plan, with extra devices billed at about 10 cents each; a one-time perpetual license is also available starting near 4,999 dollars. Organizations that prefer not to manage infrastructure can use ThingsBoard Cloud, a fully hosted SaaS billed by device and data-point volume, with a free tier covering 5 devices and 1 million data points per month, scaling up through Prototype, Pilot, Startup and Business plans to roughly 749 dollars per month for 1,000 devices and a billion monthly data points. For the largest deployments, ThingsBoard also offers a managed Private Cloud tier starting near 1,499 dollars per month for 5,000 devices, up to a custom Enterprise plan for 100,000-plus devices. ThingsBoard is commonly compared to other IoT platforms such as AWS IoT Core and Azure IoT Hub, and its main differentiator is the combination of a fully free, unrestricted open-source core with a clear, published upgrade path to commercial support and hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is ThingsBoard
paragraphs: ThingsBoard is an open-source IoT platform for connecting, managing and visualizing data from internet-connected devices. First released in 2016 and formalized as a company, ThingsBoard Inc, in 2018, it is headquartered in New York and maintains a large open-source Community Edition alongside several commercial deployment options.

The platform is used by teams building IoT products and internal fleets alike, from hardware startups self-hosting a free backend to enterprises running fully managed IoT dashboards through ThingsBoard's paid cloud and private cloud offerings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, ThingsBoard connects devices over MQTT, HTTP and CoAP, then processes incoming telemetry through a visual rule engine that can trigger alarms, transformations or downstream actions. Multi-tenancy lets a single deployment serve multiple customers or business units in isolation, and ThingsBoard Edge extends processing to gateways and edge hardware.

For building user-facing dashboards, ThingsBoard ships with a library of more than 600 customizable widgets covering charts, maps, gauges and control panels, so teams can assemble IoT interfaces without building visualization components from scratch. Device provisioning and management tools round out the platform for teams operating large device fleets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and open source under Apache 2.0, with no limit on the number of devices, assets or messages, making it a genuinely usable option for production deployments with community-only support.

Paid options scale from the self-managed Professional Edition, starting around 10 dollars per month for a 10-device Maker plan up to roughly 499 dollars per month for 1,000 devices on Business, to the fully hosted ThingsBoard Cloud, which has its own free tier and paid plans up to about 749 dollars per month, and a managed Private Cloud tier starting near 1,499 dollars per month for the largest deployments, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

features.title: Apache 2.0 Community Edition
features.description: Free, open-source core platform with no device or message limits.

features.title: MQTT/HTTP/CoAP Device Connectivity
features.description: Connects and ingests telemetry from devices over multiple standard IoT protocols.

features.title: Visual Rule Engine
features.description: Processes telemetry and triggers alarms or downstream actions through a visual rule chain builder.

features.title: 600-Plus Customizable Dashboard Widgets
features.description: A large library of prebuilt widgets for building end-user IoT dashboards.

features.title: Multi-Tenancy
features.description: Supports isolating multiple tenants or business units within one deployment.

features.title: Edge Computing
features.description: ThingsBoard Edge extends processing and rule execution to gateways and edge hardware.

features.title: Device Management and Provisioning
features.description: Tools for provisioning, organizing and managing large fleets of connected devices.

features.title: Fully Hosted Cloud Option
features.description: ThingsBoard Cloud offers a managed SaaS deployment billed by device and data-point volume.

pros: Genuinely free, unlimited-device open-source Community Edition
Clear published pricing for Professional Edition and Cloud tiers
Large library of prebuilt dashboard widgets
Supports self-hosted, managed cloud and private cloud deployment models
Apache 2.0 license allows commercial use without licensing fees
cons: Community Edition lacks white-labeling, advanced role management and Trendz Analytics
Self-hosting requires infrastructure and DevOps expertise
Cloud pricing can escalate quickly at high device or data-point volumes
Professional Edition still requires manual server management, unlike full SaaS
Some enterprise features are only available on Private Cloud or Enterprise plans

faqs.question: Is ThingsBoard free?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free and open source under Apache 2.0 with no device limits; paid tiers add support and enterprise features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ThingsBoard Community and Professional Edition?
faqs.answer: Professional Edition adds white labeling, advanced role management, Trendz Analytics and edge modules on top of the free Community Edition.

faqs.question: Does ThingsBoard offer a hosted cloud option?
faqs.answer: Yes, ThingsBoard Cloud is a fully managed SaaS billed by device and data-point volume.

faqs.question: What license does ThingsBoard use?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is licensed under Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: How many devices can Community Edition support?
faqs.answer: There is no device limit on the free Community Edition.

faqs.question: What protocols does ThingsBoard support?
faqs.answer: ThingsBoard supports MQTT, HTTP and CoAP for device connectivity.

faqs.question: Who makes ThingsBoard?
faqs.answer: ThingsBoard is developed by ThingsBoard Inc, founded in 2016 and headquartered in New York.

faqs.question: What is ThingsBoard Edge?
faqs.answer: ThingsBoard Edge extends the platform's processing capabilities to gateways and edge hardware.

use_cases.description: A hardware startup self-hosts the free Community Edition as the backend for its connected device fleet.

use_cases.title: White-Labeled Enterprise Dashboards
use_cases.description: An enterprise uses the Professional Edition to deliver white-labeled IoT dashboards to internal teams or customers.

use_cases.title: Fully Managed IoT SaaS
use_cases.description: A company avoids managing infrastructure by running its IoT solution entirely on ThingsBoard Cloud.

enrichment.short_description: Thinkific is a publicly traded (TSX: THNC) Vancouver-founded platform for creating and selling online courses, communities, memberships and coaching, used by independent creators and small businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Thinkific Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Thinkific lets creators sell online courses, communities and memberships. Compare its Basic, Start, Grow and Plus pricing plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Thinkific is an online course and learning-commerce platform founded in 2012 in Vancouver, Canada by Greg Smith, Matt Payne and Miranda Lievers. The company went public on the Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker THNC and today serves tens of thousands of course creators, coaches and small businesses who have collectively generated billions of dollars in sales through the platform. Thinkific lets users build and sell online courses, cohort-based communities, memberships, digital downloads, live coaching and webinars from a single branded site, without needing separate video hosting, payment processing or community software. Core platform capabilities include a drag-and-drop course builder, customizable website themes, quizzes and certificates, a built-in community and discussion feature, live lesson and coaching scheduling, email marketing and automation tools, and native or third-party checkout with, on paid plans, 0 percent Thinkific transaction fees in primary markets like the United States. As of 2026 Thinkific no longer offers a permanently free plan, instead giving new users a 14-day free trial before requiring a paid subscription; published tiers are Basic at 36 dollars per month billed annually or 49 dollars month-to-month, Start at 74 dollars per month annually or 99 dollars monthly, and Grow at 149 dollars per month annually or 199 dollars monthly, each unlocking more advanced marketing, community and integration features plus higher limits, alongside unlimited courses and students on every tier. Above Grow sits Thinkific Plus, a custom-priced enterprise tier covering multi-site management, CRM integrations such as Salesforce and HubSpot, bulk B2B selling, SCORM compliance, single sign-on and dedicated success management. Thinkific's main competitors are Kajabi, Teachable and Podia, and it is generally positioned as the more scalable, feature-complete option once a creator's course business grows beyond a hobby-level catalog.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Thinkific
paragraphs: Thinkific is an online learning-commerce platform founded in 2012 in Vancouver, Canada by Greg Smith, Matt Payne and Miranda Lievers, and it is now a publicly traded company on the Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker THNC. The platform lets creators, coaches and small businesses build and sell online courses, communities, memberships and coaching products from one branded site.

Thinkific has grown from a simple course-hosting tool into a broader learning-commerce platform, adding communities, live coaching, memberships and digital downloads alongside its original course-building features, aimed at helping course creators diversify revenue beyond one-off course sales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of Thinkific is a drag-and-drop course builder combined with customizable website themes, quizzes, certificates and completion tracking, so creators can build a full branded learning site without code. A built-in community and discussion feature lets creators run cohort-based or ongoing communities alongside their courses.

Beyond courses, Thinkific supports memberships, digital downloads, live lessons and coaching scheduling, plus email marketing automation to nurture leads and re-engage students. On paid plans, Thinkific charges 0 percent transaction fees in primary markets like the United States, meaning creators keep more of each sale compared with platforms that take a cut per transaction.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: As of 2026, Thinkific no longer offers a permanently free plan, instead giving new users a 14-day free trial. Published self-serve plans are Basic at 36 dollars per month billed annually or 49 dollars month-to-month, Start at 74 dollars per month annually or 99 dollars monthly, and Grow at 149 dollars per month annually or 199 dollars monthly, with unlimited courses and students included on every tier.

Above Grow sits Thinkific Plus, a custom-priced enterprise tier for organizations that need multi-site management, CRM integrations, bulk selling and enterprise support; pricing for Plus is quoted directly by Thinkific's sales team rather than published.

Public company with a long operating history since 2012
14-day trial lets creators test the platform before committing
cons: No permanently free plan as of 2026, unlike some competitors

Plus tier pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Advanced automation and integrations are mostly reserved for higher tiers

faqs.question: What is Thinkific used for?

faqs.answer: Plans start at 36 dollars per month billed annually for Basic, up to 149 dollars per month for Grow, plus a custom-priced Plus tier.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, as of 2026 Thinkific offers a 14-day free trial instead of a permanently free plan.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Thinkific Basic, Start and Grow?
faqs.answer: Each tier unlocks more marketing, community and integration features and higher limits, with Grow offering the most advanced tools among the self-serve plans.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: No, paid Thinkific plans charge 0 percent transaction fees in primary markets like the United States.

faqs.question: What is Thinkific Plus?

faqs.answer: Yes, Thinkific trades on the Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker THNC.

faqs.question: Who are Thinkific's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Thinkific is most often compared to Kajabi, Teachable and Podia.

enrichment.short_description: Thinkific Plus is the custom-priced enterprise tier of Thinkific, the Vancouver-founded, publicly traded online course platform, built for larger organizations running corporate training or customer education at scale; it is the same company as Thinkific, not a separate competitor.
enrichment.seo_title: Thinkific Plus Review 2026: Enterprise Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Thinkific Plus is Thinkific's enterprise LMS tier for corporate training and customer education. See its multi-site, CRM and SSO features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Thinkific Plus is the enterprise-grade tier of Thinkific, the Vancouver, Canada-founded and publicly traded (TSX: THNC) online course and learning-commerce platform originally launched in 2012 by Greg Smith, Matt Payne and Miranda Lievers. Where the standard Thinkific plans, Basic, Start and Grow, target individual creators and small businesses, Thinkific Plus is built for larger organizations that need to run corporate training, partner or customer education, or multi-brand course businesses at scale, and it is sold entirely on custom, quote-based pricing rather than published monthly tiers. Key capabilities that separate Plus from the standard product include support for multiple Thinkific sites managed from one organization, so a company can organize courses by department, brand or audience while keeping shared administration; native CRM integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot and similar platforms to sync learner data into sales and marketing workflows; bulk-sell checkout for selling course access in volume to other organizations or corporate clients; SCORM compliance and learning-path sequencing for structured corporate training; single sign-on for enterprise identity providers; an AI teaching assistant; enterprise-grade API access; and dedicated onboarding plus a named customer success manager rather than standard-tier support queues. Because every Thinkific Plus contract is custom-quoted based on student volume, number of sites, and required integrations, prospective customers must contact Thinkific's sales team directly to get pricing, and the company does not publish a starting price the way it does for its self-serve Basic, Start and Grow plans. Thinkific Plus competes with enterprise learning platforms such as Docebo and LearnUpon, and with corporate LMS vendors like Cornerstone OnDemand, differentiating itself through its roots in Thinkific's creator-friendly course-building tools combined with enterprise administration and integration features layered on top.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Thinkific Plus
paragraphs: Thinkific Plus is the enterprise tier of Thinkific, the same Vancouver-founded, publicly traded company behind the standard Thinkific course platform. Rather than being a separate product from a different vendor, Plus is a custom-configured version of Thinkific built for organizations running training or education programs at a scale beyond what the self-serve Basic, Start and Grow plans are designed for.

Typical Thinkific Plus customers include companies delivering corporate onboarding and compliance training, software vendors running customer education programs, and multi-brand or franchise organizations that need to manage several course sites under one account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Thinkific Plus adds multi-site management so an organization can run several Thinkific sites, organized by brand, department or audience, from shared administration. Native CRM integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot and similar platforms sync learner activity into sales and marketing systems, and bulk-sell checkout supports selling course access in volume to corporate or partner clients.

For corporate training use cases, Plus adds SCORM compliance, learning-path sequencing, single sign-on for enterprise identity providers, an AI teaching assistant, and enterprise-grade API access. Plus customers also get dedicated onboarding and a named customer success manager rather than standard support queues.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Thinkific Plus does not publish flat pricing. Every contract is custom-quoted based on factors like student volume, number of managed sites, and which integrations and enterprise features the organization needs, so prospective customers must contact Thinkific's sales team directly for a quote.

This differs from the standard Thinkific plans, which publish fixed monthly and annual prices for Basic, Start and Grow; Plus sits above Grow specifically for organizations whose needs, and budgets, exceed what a fixed self-serve tier can support.

features.description: Named customer success manager and guided onboarding for large accounts.

Multi-site support suits organizations with multiple brands or departments
Native CRM integrations streamline learner-to-lead workflows
SCORM and SSO support fit corporate training and compliance needs
Dedicated success manager and onboarding for large accounts
cons: No published pricing, so budgeting requires a sales conversation
Overkill and cost-prohibitive for solo creators or very small teams
Requires coordinating with or migrating from standard Thinkific plans
Enterprise procurement and contracting can slow down implementation
Fewer independently published reviews than the standard Thinkific product

faqs.question: What is Thinkific Plus?
faqs.answer: It is the custom-priced enterprise tier of Thinkific, built for larger organizations running training or education programs at scale.

faqs.question: How is Thinkific Plus different from regular Thinkific?
faqs.answer: Plus adds multi-site management, CRM integrations, SCORM compliance, SSO and dedicated support beyond what the Basic, Start and Grow plans offer.

faqs.question: How much does Thinkific Plus cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is custom and quoted directly by Thinkific's sales team based on the organization's needs.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific Plus support multiple sites?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plus allows an organization to manage multiple Thinkific sites under one account.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific Plus integrate with Salesforce or HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Yes, Thinkific Plus includes native CRM integrations with platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific Plus support SCORM content?
faqs.answer: Yes, SCORM compliance is included for structured corporate training content.

faqs.question: Who is Thinkific Plus for?
faqs.answer: It is aimed at enterprises, software companies and multi-brand organizations running training or customer education at scale.

faqs.question: Does Thinkific Plus include single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, single sign-on for enterprise identity providers is included in Thinkific Plus.
